metadata.career_relevance: Wildcard signatures appear throughout Java's standard library: `Collections.copy(List<? super T>, List<? extends T>)`, `Collections.sort(List<T extends Comparable<? super T>>)`, `Stream.flatMap(Function<? super T, ? extends Stream<? extends R>>)`. Without understanding wildcards, these signatures are impenetrable. With wildcards, you can read and write flexible generic APIs. After this topic, the last cryptic piece of Java generics becomes clear.

hook.real_world_problem:
You write a method to print all numbers in a list:

```java
void printAll(List<Number> numbers) {
    for (Number n : numbers) System.out.println(n);
}
```

Looks fine — until you call it:
```java
List<Integer> ints    = Arrays.asList(1, 2, 3);
List<Double>  doubles = Arrays.asList(1.1, 2.2);

printAll(ints);    // COMPILE ERROR!
printAll(doubles); // COMPILE ERROR!
```

But Integer IS-A Number and Double IS-A Number! Why doesn't this work?

**Because generics are INVARIANT.** `List<Integer>` is NOT a `List<Number>` — even though Integer extends Number. The type relationship doesn't carry through the generic.

*Why?* Safety. If `List<Integer>` were a `List<Number>`, you could do:
```java
void addNumber(List<Number> list) { list.add(3.14); }  // adds a Double!
List<Integer> ints = new ArrayList<>();
addNumber(ints);  // now ints has a Double — runtime explosion!
```

**The wildcard solution:**
```java
// ? extends Number = any List of Number or any subtype of Number
void printAll(List<? extends Number> numbers) {  // READ ONLY
    for (Number n : numbers) System.out.println(n);
}

printAll(ints);    // OK — List<Integer> satisfies ? extends Number
printAll(doubles); // OK — List<Double> satisfies ? extends Number
```

Today you learn the `?` wildcard — Java's way to say 'a List of something, but I don't know exactly what.'
hook.what_you_will_build: By the end of this topic, you'll understand why generics are invariant, use unbounded `?`, upper-bounded `? extends T`, and lower-bounded `? super T` wildcards, apply the PECS rule (Producer Extends, Consumer Super), and read Java library wildcard signatures confidently.

visual_aid.description: Three stacked diagrams: (1) Invariance: Number hierarchy (Integer → Number → Object) on left; List hierarchy on right — List<Integer>, List<Number>, List<Object> are INDEPENDENT (no arrows between them). (2) ? extends Number: a wide funnel accepting List<Integer>, List<Double>, List<Long>, List<Float>, List<Number> — all pour in. Data flows OUT (arrow labeled READING ONLY). X on adding. (3) ? super Integer: a funnel accepting List<Integer>, List<Number>, List<Object>. Data flows IN (arrow labeled WRITING ONLY, Integer goes in). Only Object comes out when reading. Bottom: PECS visual — data flowing OUT = extends; data flowing IN = super.
visual_aid.alt_text: Invariance, upper bounded wildcard funnel, lower bounded wildcard funnel, and PECS data flow

worked_example.scenario: A real Java utility: implement `Collections.copy()`-style methods and a `merge()`-style method using wildcards to make them as flexible as possible. Then demonstrate reading Java's own wildcard signatures.
worked_example.problem_statement:
Implement three wildcard-driven utility methods:
1. `sumAll(List<? extends Number>)` — sum any numeric list
2. `addDefaults(List<? super String>, String...)` — add strings to any compatible list
3. `copy(List<? super T>, List<? extends T>)` — copy from source to destination

Then decode Collections.sort's signature.
worked_example.expected_output:
60.0
7.0
300.0
[Aisha, Raj, Priya]
[1, 2, 3]
Size: 3, items: [Aisha, Raj, Priya]
Size: 3, items: [1, 2, 3]
things_to_notice: sumAll accepts List<Integer>, List<Double>, AND List<Long> — all from the same method declaration. Without wildcards, you'd need three overloads or use the raw List<Number> (which doesn't accept List<Integer>).
fillDefaults uses `? super String` — it accepts List<String>, List<Object>, List<CharSequence>. The key: we're WRITING Strings into it, so any list that CAN hold Strings is valid.
copy is the PECS pattern in action: src is `? extends T` (we READ from it — it produces T values), dest is `? super T` (we WRITE to it — it consumes T values). T ties them together.
printInfo with unbounded `?` — we only need size() and iteration as Object. We don't care about the element type at all.
The copy call: `copy(numbers, ints)` — T is inferred as Integer. List<Number> satisfies `? super Integer` (Number IS a supertype of Integer). List<Integer> satisfies `? extends Integer`.

industry_examples.use_case: `Collections.copy(List<? super T> dest, List<? extends T> src)` — the canonical PECS example. `Collections.sort(List<T extends Comparable<? super T>>)` — the `? super T` allows sorting a `List<Integer>` using a `Comparator<Number>`. Without wildcards, sort would need exact type matches.

`Stream.map(Function<? super T, ? extends R>)` — the function parameter is `? super T` (consumer of T) and `? extends R` (producer of R). This allows passing a `Function<Object, String>` where `Function<Integer, String>` is expected — the function can handle Integer (which is Object). Wildcards make the Stream API far more flexible.

industry_examples.company_context: Spring Framework — event system, strategy patterns
industry_examples.use_case: Spring's ApplicationContext.publishEvent(Object event) and listeners using `ApplicationListener<? extends ApplicationEvent>`. Spring MVC's HandlerMethodArgumentResolver uses `List<? extends HandlerMethodArgumentResolver>`. Wildcards allow Spring to work with any concrete event/resolver subtype through generic collections.

applications.interview_relevance:
Wildcard questions at senior level:

- **'Why is List<Integer> not a List<Number>?'** — Generics are invariant. If allowed, you could add a Double to a List<Integer> through the List<Number> reference.
- **'What is PECS?'** — Producer Extends, Consumer Super. Read from a collection → `? extends T`. Write to a collection → `? super T`.
- **'What is the difference between `List<?>` and `List<Object>`?'** → `List<Object>` only accepts `List<Object>`. `List<?>` accepts any list. You can add to `List<Object>`; you can't add to `List<?>`.
- **'Explain Collections.copy signature'** → `<T> void copy(List<? super T> dest, List<? extends T> src)`. src is a producer (extends = producer), dest is a consumer (super = consumer). T ties them: copy works when src element type is a subtype of dest element type.
applications.real_world_workflow:
Daily wildcard usage:

- API method accepting any numeric list: `void process(List<? extends Number> nums)`
- Method that adds to a collection: `void populate(List<? super T> dest)`
- Utility reading any collection: `int size(Collection<?> c)` — or just use Collection
- Interpreting Spring/library signatures: `List<? extends HandlerInterceptor>` → producers
- Writing flexible generic utility methods that work for whole type families

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Trying to add elements to a `List<? extends T>` — compile error.
common_gaps.remediation: ? extends = PRODUCER = READ ONLY. You cannot add to it. If you need to add, use `List<? super T>` or a named type parameter.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: Compile error: cannot add to type-safe List? extends

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Trying to read a specific type from `List<? super Integer>` — only Object available.
common_gaps.remediation: ? super = CONSUMER = WRITE ONLY (reads give Object). If you need typed reads, use `? extends T` or a named type parameter.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: Compile error assigning list.get(0) to a typed variable from ? super

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Using `List<Object>` where flexibility is needed — doesn't accept List<String>, List<Integer>.
common_gaps.remediation: `List<Object>` only accepts List<Object>. Use `List<?>` for any list or `List<? extends SomeType>` for lists of a type family.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: Passing List<String> to parameter of type List<Object> — compile error

key_takeaways: Generics are INVARIANT: List<Integer> is NOT a List<Number> — even though Integer extends Number
Unbounded `?`: accepts any List; can read as Object; cannot add
Upper bounded `? extends T`: accepts List<T> and List<subtypes of T>; READ as T; cannot add
Lower bounded `? super T`: accepts List<T> and List<supertypes of T>; ADD T; read only as Object
Data flows OUT → Producer → ? extends; Data flows IN → Consumer → ? super
Named type parameter needed when: return type uses T, or multiple params must agree on T
Wildcard when: T appears only once in the method signature, you just need flexibility
List<?> vs List<Object>: List<Object> accepts only List<Object>; List<?> accepts any List
Collections.copy: ? extends (producer src), ? super (consumer dest) — the PECS textbook example

self_check_questions.question:
A method is defined as `void process(List<? extends Shape> shapes)`. Which calls compile?
a) `process(new ArrayList<Circle>())`
b) `process(new ArrayList<Shape>())`
c) `process(new ArrayList<Object>())`
self_check_questions.answer: a) Compiles — Circle extends Shape, satisfies ? extends Shape. b) Compiles — Shape satisfies ? extends Shape (extends includes the type itself). c) COMPILE ERROR — Object does NOT extend Shape; it's the other direction.

self_check_questions.question: What is PECS and when do you apply each part?
self_check_questions.answer: PECS = Producer Extends, Consumer Super. Apply by direction of data flow: If data flows OUT of a collection (you read/iterate it), use `? extends T` — the collection PRODUCES T values. If data flows INTO a collection (you add to it), use `? super T` — the collection CONSUMES T values. Example: copying from a list of Integers to a list of Numbers — source (produces Integers) uses `? extends Integer`, destination (consumes Integers) uses `? super Integer`.

self_check_questions.question: What is the difference between `List<?>` and `List<Object>`?
self_check_questions.answer: `List<Object>`: a specific parameterized type — only accepts `ArrayList<Object>` or other `List<Object>`. You CAN add to it (list.add(anything)). `List<?>`: an unbounded wildcard — accepts ANY list regardless of element type (List<String>, List<Integer>, List<SomeClass>). You CANNOT add to it (compiler doesn't know what type would be safe). Use `List<?>` when you need to accept any list but only need container operations (size, clear, iterate as Object). Use `List<Object>` when you specifically want a list of Objects that can hold mixed types.

reflection.next_topic_preview: Final Module 9 topic: **Generic Interfaces and Design Patterns** — writing generic interfaces, implementing them, and the design patterns they enable: generic Repository pattern, generic Strategy pattern, generic Builder pattern. After Topic 9.5, you understand generics as a design tool — not just syntax. You'll see how `Repository<T, ID>`, `Mapper<S, T>`, `Builder<T>` are built and why they make professional APIs so clean.
